statusLine: EU fuel reserves sit below mandatory minimums in a majority of member states, placing overall supply security under strain heading into summer 2026.
fullAnalysis:
As of mid-June 2026, the EU's aggregate oil stock position registers a 'watch' status, with average reserves of 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87.0 days for jet fuel. While the petrol average nominally clears the 90-day mandatory minimum, both diesel and jet fuel fall short at the EU level — a significant concern given that diesel underpins freight and logistics across the continent, and jet fuel demand is at its seasonal peak. These averages, however, obscure a deeply uneven picture across member states.

At the country level, the strain is acute: 17 of 27 member states hold at least one fuel category in critical status — meaning reserves fall well below the 90-day threshold. Romania's diesel stock stands at just 30.2 days, Poland's jet fuel at 22.5 days, and Portugal's jet fuel at 21.2 days — figures that represent severe shortfalls. Central and southern European countries are disproportionately affected, with Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal each registering critical status across all three fuel types. The partial drawdown of strategic reserves in response to Middle East supply disruptions has visibly eroded buffer capacity in these markets, and replenishment has not kept pace with consumption.

Jet fuel is the most structurally exposed category. Even countries with otherwise adequate petrol and diesel stocks — including Denmark (75.7 days), Finland (31.9 days), Latvia (51.6 days), and the Netherlands (63.2 days) — are carrying critical jet fuel inventories. With the summer aviation season now underway, this represents an elevated risk to flight operations and airline cost structures, particularly if crude supply tightens further. Brent crude has risen 12.8% since late February 2026, from $72.48 to $81.73 per barrel, and while today's session saw a $2.01 decline, the underlying upward trend since the escalation onset has not reversed.

On the price side, EU average petrol stands at €1.786 per litre and diesel at €1.804 per litre as of 8 June. The spread across member states is substantial: Danish drivers pay €2.392 per litre for petrol while Maltese consumers pay €1.340 — a gap of over €1.05 per litre reflecting differences in taxation, subsidy policy, and supply chain exposure rather than crude price alone. The combination of rising crude costs, depleted strategic buffers in key economies, and a diesel and jet fuel average below the mandatory 90-day floor suggests the EU's fuel security position warrants close monitoring in the weeks ahead.
keyPoints: EU diesel (88.1 days) and jet fuel (87.0 days) averages both fall below the 90-day mandatory minimum, while petrol (95.6 days) marginally clears the threshold.
17 of 27 member states hold at least one fuel type in critical status; 5 of 27 — Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal — are critical across all three fuel categories.

Jet fuel is the most widespread vulnerability: countries including Finland (31.9 days), Poland (22.5 days), and Portugal (21.2 days) carry critically low aviation fuel stocks at the peak of summer travel season.

Brent crude has risen 12.8% since late February 2026 ($72.48 to $81.73/bbl), reflecting sustained pressure linked to Middle East supply disruptions that have also prompted strategic reserve releases across several member states.
Petrol prices vary by more than €1.05 per litre across the EU — from €1.340 in Malta to €2.392 in Denmark — highlighting divergent national policy responses to the current supply environment.
